title: Run-on Sentences & Comma Splice Mistakes - 50 Mistakes
definition: A run-on sentence happens when two complete sentences are joined together with no punctuation or the wrong punctuation - a comma splice is a specific type where two complete sentences are joined by just a comma, which isn't strong enough to hold them together. Here are 50 common examples, explained simply, with several ways to fix each one.

commonMistakes.wrong: I went to the store I bought some milk.
commonMistakes.right: I went to the store, and I bought some milk.
commonMistakes.why: These are two complete sentences (each has its own subject and verb) joined with no punctuation at all, which is called a run-on sentence. Fix it by adding a comma plus a joining word (and, but, so), or by using a period or semicolon to separate them into two sentences.

commonMistakes.wrong: She was tired, she went to bed early.
commonMistakes.right: She was tired, so she went to bed early.
commonMistakes.why: This is a comma splice - two complete sentences joined by only a comma, with no connecting word. A comma alone isn't strong enough to join two full sentences; add a joining word like 'so', 'and', or 'but' after the comma, or replace the comma with a period or semicolon.

commonMistakes.wrong: It was raining, we stayed inside.
commonMistakes.right: It was raining, so we stayed inside.
commonMistakes.why: Just like the previous example, two complete ideas are joined with only a comma - this needs a connecting word to show the relationship between them (here, cause and effect). Adding 'so' shows that the rain caused them to stay inside.

commonMistakes.wrong: I like coffee, she likes tea.
commonMistakes.right: I like coffee, but she likes tea.
commonMistakes.why: This comma splice needs a contrasting word like 'but' to show the two people have different preferences. Whenever two complete sentences show a contrast, join them with a comma plus 'but', not a comma alone.

commonMistakes.wrong: He studied hard, he still failed the exam.
commonMistakes.right: He studied hard, but he still failed the exam.
commonMistakes.why: Since the second idea contrasts with the first (studying hard usually leads to passing, but here it didn't), 'but' is the correct connector. Always check whether your two ideas agree or contrast to choose between 'and' and 'but'.

commonMistakes.wrong: The movie was long it was very boring.
commonMistakes.right: The movie was long, and it was very boring.
commonMistakes.why: Two complete sentences here are joined with no punctuation at all - add a comma and 'and' since both ideas support each other (long AND boring). Remember: a comma alone can never join two full sentences; it always needs a joining word with it (unless using a semicolon).

commonMistakes.wrong: I finished my homework, I watched TV.
commonMistakes.right: I finished my homework, and then I watched TV. / I finished my homework; then I watched TV.
commonMistakes.why: This comma splice can be fixed by adding 'and then' to show the sequence of events, or by using a semicolon to join the two related sentences. Semicolons work well when two sentences are closely related in meaning but don't need a specific joining word.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is smart, she works very hard.
commonMistakes.right: She is smart, and she works very hard.
commonMistakes.why: Both ideas support each other positively here, so 'and' is the correct connector after the comma. Whenever both parts of a sentence add similar or supporting information, 'and' is usually the right choice.

commonMistakes.wrong: I wanted to go, I didn't have time.
commonMistakes.right: I wanted to go, but I didn't have time.
commonMistakes.why: Since the second part contrasts with the first (wanting to go, but not having time), 'but' is needed to correctly join these two complete sentences. This is a very common pattern in everyday speech, so practicing the correct comma + but structure is valuable.

commonMistakes.wrong: The weather was cold, we wore jackets.
commonMistakes.right: The weather was cold, so we wore jackets.
commonMistakes.why: The second sentence shows the RESULT of the first (cold weather caused jacket-wearing), so 'so' is the correct connecting word. Whenever one idea causes or leads to the next, use a comma plus 'so'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have a lot of work, I can't come to the party.
commonMistakes.right: I have a lot of work, so I can't come to the party.
commonMistakes.why: Again, the second part is the RESULT of the first (a lot of work leads to not being able to attend), so 'so' fits correctly. Practice identifying cause-and-effect relationships to know when 'so' is the right connector.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is a good friend, he always helps me.
commonMistakes.right: He is a good friend, and he always helps me.
commonMistakes.why: Both statements support the same positive idea about this person, so 'and' correctly joins them. When both parts of your sentence point in the same direction (both positive or both negative), 'and' usually works well.

commonMistakes.wrong: I called her twice, she didn't answer.
commonMistakes.right: I called her twice, but she didn't answer.
commonMistakes.why: The second part contrasts with the expected outcome of the first (calling twice, but still no answer), so 'but' is correct. This shows an unexpected or disappointing result, which 'but' signals clearly.

commonMistakes.wrong: The traffic was heavy, I arrived late.
commonMistakes.right: The traffic was heavy, so I arrived late.
commonMistakes.why: Since arriving late is the RESULT of heavy traffic, 'so' is the correct connecting word here. Cause-and-effect relationships almost always need 'so' to link them properly with a comma.

commonMistakes.wrong: She loves music, she plays the piano every day.
commonMistakes.right: She loves music, and she plays the piano every day.
commonMistakes.why: Both statements support the same idea (her passion for music), making 'and' the correct choice. Whenever your two sentences build on the same overall point, 'and' ties them together smoothly.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am tired, I need to rest.
commonMistakes.right: I am tired, so I need to rest.
commonMistakes.why: The need to rest is a direct RESULT of being tired, so 'so' correctly shows this cause-and-effect relationship. This is one of the most natural, common sentence patterns in daily English, worth practicing directly.

commonMistakes.wrong: He apologized, she forgave him.
commonMistakes.right: He apologized, and she forgave him. / He apologized, so she forgave him.
commonMistakes.why: Both 'and' (simply listing two related events) and 'so' (showing his apology caused her forgiveness) work here, depending on the exact relationship you want to express. Choose 'so' if you want to emphasize cause-and-effect, or 'and' for a simple sequence.

commonMistakes.wrong: I don't like spicy food, my brother loves it.
commonMistakes.right: I don't like spicy food, but my brother loves it.
commonMistakes.why: Since this shows a contrast between two people's preferences, 'but' is the correct connecting word. Whenever you compare two different opinions or preferences, 'but' signals that contrast clearly.

commonMistakes.wrong: We left early, we wanted to avoid traffic.
commonMistakes.right: We left early because we wanted to avoid traffic. / We left early to avoid traffic.
commonMistakes.why: Here, the second part explains the REASON for the first action, so 'because' (not just a comma) is the clearest connector. Whenever the second part explains 'why' the first part happened, use 'because' to join them properly.

commonMistakes.wrong: It started to rain, we went inside quickly.
commonMistakes.right: It started to rain, so we went inside quickly.
commonMistakes.why: Going inside is the RESULT of the rain starting, making 'so' the correct choice to link these two events. This is another clear cause-and-effect pattern, similar to several examples already covered.

commonMistakes.wrong: I read the instructions carefully, I still made a mistake.
commonMistakes.right: I read the instructions carefully, but I still made a mistake.
commonMistakes.why: The unexpected outcome here (still making a mistake despite careful reading) needs 'but' to show the contrast. Whenever something happens despite an effort, use 'but' to connect the two ideas.

commonMistakes.wrong: She practiced every day, she became an expert.
commonMistakes.right: She practiced every day, and she became an expert. / She practiced every day, so she became an expert.
commonMistakes.why: Depending on emphasis, both 'and' (simple sequence) and 'so' (result of practice) can work correctly here. Consider what relationship you want to highlight - simple listing, or clear cause and effect.

commonMistakes.wrong: I saved money for a year, I bought a new phone.
commonMistakes.right: I saved money for a year, so I bought a new phone.
commonMistakes.why: Buying the phone is the direct RESULT of saving money, so 'so' correctly connects these two complete sentences. This pattern - saving leading to buying - is a classic cause-and-effect relationship.

commonMistakes.wrong: The book was interesting, I finished it in one day.
commonMistakes.right: The book was interesting, so I finished it in one day.
commonMistakes.why: Finishing the book quickly is a RESULT of it being interesting, making 'so' the correct connector. Whenever an outcome directly follows from a quality or condition, use 'so'.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is very talented, he never practices.
commonMistakes.right: He is very talented, but he never practices.
commonMistakes.why: This shows a surprising contrast (talent without practice), so 'but' correctly links these ideas. Contrast words like 'but' are essential whenever two facts seem to work against each other.

commonMistakes.wrong: I wanted to help, I didn't know how.
commonMistakes.right: I wanted to help, but I didn't know how.
commonMistakes.why: The second part contrasts with the intention expressed in the first (wanting to help, but lacking knowledge on how), so 'but' fits correctly. This pattern of good intention followed by a limitation is very common in daily conversation.

commonMistakes.wrong: She sent the email, she forgot the attachment.
commonMistakes.right: She sent the email, but she forgot the attachment.
commonMistakes.why: Forgetting the attachment is an unfortunate contrast to the action of sending the email, so 'but' correctly shows this. Whenever a mistake or oversight follows an action, 'but' usually links them well.

commonMistakes.wrong: I studied all night, I still felt unprepared.
commonMistakes.right: I studied all night, but I still felt unprepared.
commonMistakes.why: The unexpected outcome (still feeling unprepared despite studying) requires 'but' to show the contrast clearly. This is a very relatable, common sentence pattern about effort not matching the expected outcome.

commonMistakes.wrong: The team worked together, they won the championship.
commonMistakes.right: The team worked together, and they won the championship. / The team worked together, so they won the championship.
commonMistakes.why: Depending on your intended emphasis, either 'and' (listing the events) or 'so' (showing teamwork caused the win) works well here. Consider whether you want to simply connect two facts, or highlight one causing the other.

commonMistakes.wrong: I missed the bus, I had to walk to work.
commonMistakes.right: I missed the bus, so I had to walk to work.
commonMistakes.why: Walking to work is the direct RESULT of missing the bus, making 'so' the correct connector. This cause-and-effect pattern appears very frequently in everyday storytelling.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is allergic to nuts, she avoids all nut products.
commonMistakes.right: She is allergic to nuts, so she avoids all nut products.
commonMistakes.why: Avoiding nut products is a direct RESULT of the allergy, so 'so' correctly connects these two complete sentences. Whenever a behavior is explained by a preceding fact or condition, 'so' usually fits.

commonMistakes.wrong: I forgot my umbrella, I got completely soaked.
commonMistakes.right: I forgot my umbrella, so I got completely soaked.
commonMistakes.why: Getting soaked is the direct RESULT of forgetting the umbrella, making 'so' the natural connector. This is another classic cause-and-effect storytelling pattern.

commonMistakes.wrong: He speaks three languages, he learned them all as a child.
commonMistakes.right: He speaks three languages, and he learned them all as a child. / He speaks three languages because he learned them all as a child.
commonMistakes.why: You can either simply connect these facts with 'and', or show the explanation more directly with 'because'. Choose 'because' if you want to emphasize WHY he speaks three languages.

commonMistakes.wrong: The store was closed, we went to a different one.
commonMistakes.right: The store was closed, so we went to a different one.
commonMistakes.why: Going to a different store is the RESULT of the first store being closed, so 'so' is correct here. This is a very typical everyday situation showing clear cause and effect.

commonMistakes.wrong: I didn't have my phone, I couldn't call for help.
commonMistakes.right: I didn't have my phone, so I couldn't call for help.
commonMistakes.why: Not being able to call for help is the direct RESULT of not having a phone, making 'so' the correct connecting word. This pattern of 'lacking something leading to an inability' is very common.

commonMistakes.wrong: She works two jobs, she is always exhausted.
commonMistakes.right: She works two jobs, so she is always exhausted.
commonMistakes.why: Being exhausted is the RESULT of working two jobs, so 'so' correctly links these two complete sentences. Whenever a state of being (tired, happy, stressed) follows logically from a situation, 'so' usually connects them well.

commonMistakes.wrong: I planted the seeds in spring, they bloomed by summer.
commonMistakes.right: I planted the seeds in spring, and they bloomed by summer.
commonMistakes.why: These two events happen in sequence and support the same overall story, so 'and' works well to connect them. When events simply happen one after another as part of a natural sequence, 'and' is often the best, simplest choice.

commonMistakes.wrong: I lost my keys, I had to call a locksmith.
commonMistakes.right: I lost my keys, so I had to call a locksmith.
commonMistakes.why: Calling a locksmith is the direct RESULT of losing the keys, so 'so' correctly connects these two ideas. This is yet another clear example of the cause-and-effect pattern that appears throughout everyday English.

commonMistakes.wrong: The recipe looked easy, it turned out to be quite difficult.
commonMistakes.right: The recipe looked easy, but it turned out to be quite difficult.
commonMistakes.why: This shows an unexpected contrast between appearance (looked easy) and reality (was difficult), so 'but' is the correct connector. Whenever expectations don't match reality, 'but' signals that surprising contrast.

commonMistakes.wrong: I exercise every morning, I feel more energetic.
commonMistakes.right: I exercise every morning, so I feel more energetic. / I exercise every morning, and I feel more energetic.
commonMistakes.why: You can use 'so' to emphasize that exercise causes the energy, or 'and' to simply list both facts together. Think about whether you want to stress the cause-and-effect relationship, or just connect two related facts.

commonMistakes.wrong: He locked the door, he left for work.
commonMistakes.right: He locked the door, and he left for work. / He locked the door before he left for work.
commonMistakes.why: These are sequential actions, so 'and' works to simply connect them, or you can use 'before' to show the specific order of events more precisely. Consider whether the order of actions matters enough to specify with 'before' or 'after'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I turned off the lights, I went to sleep.
commonMistakes.right: I turned off the lights, and then I went to sleep.
commonMistakes.why: Adding 'and then' clarifies the sequence of these two actions, fixing the comma splice while showing the order clearly. Whenever actions happen one after another, 'and then' is a natural, clear way to connect them.

commonMistakes.wrong: She checked her email, she replied to three messages.
commonMistakes.right: She checked her email, and she replied to three messages.
commonMistakes.why: These two actions are simply sequential parts of the same activity, so 'and' correctly joins them. When describing a series of related steps someone took, 'and' is usually the simplest, most natural connector.

commonMistakes.wrong: The company grew quickly, they hired many new employees.
commonMistakes.right: The company grew quickly, so they hired many new employees.
commonMistakes.why: Hiring new employees is the RESULT of quick growth, so 'so' is the correct word to connect these two complete sentences. This is a common business-related cause-and-effect pattern.

commonMistakes.wrong: I read the review, I decided not to watch the movie.
commonMistakes.right: I read the review, so I decided not to watch the movie.
commonMistakes.why: The decision not to watch is a direct RESULT of reading the review, making 'so' the correct connector. This shows how a review (the cause) led to a decision (the effect).

commonMistakes.wrong: The internet was down, we couldn't finish the meeting online.
commonMistakes.right: The internet was down, so we couldn't finish the meeting online.
commonMistakes.why: The inability to finish the meeting is the RESULT of the internet outage, so 'so' correctly links these ideas. This is another everyday technology-related cause-and-effect example.

commonMistakes.wrong: He trained for months, he finally ran the marathon.
commonMistakes.right: He trained for months, and he finally ran the marathon.
commonMistakes.why: These two facts build on the same overall achievement story, so 'and' works well to connect them here. When both parts of a sentence build toward the same accomplishment, 'and' ties them together naturally.

commonMistakes.wrong: I checked the weather forecast, I decided to bring an umbrella.
commonMistakes.right: I checked the weather forecast, so I decided to bring an umbrella.
commonMistakes.why: Deciding to bring an umbrella is the direct RESULT of checking the forecast, making 'so' the correct connector. This everyday decision-making pattern appears constantly in daily conversation.

commonMistakes.wrong: The flight was delayed, we missed our connecting flight.
commonMistakes.right: The flight was delayed, so we missed our connecting flight.
commonMistakes.why: Missing the connection is the direct RESULT of the delay, so 'so' correctly joins these two complete sentences. This travel-related example shows a very relatable everyday frustration.

commonMistakes.wrong: I proofread my essay twice, I still found an error.
commonMistakes.right: I proofread my essay twice, but I still found an error.
commonMistakes.why: This shows a surprising contrast (proofreading carefully, yet still finding a mistake), so 'but' correctly connects these ideas. Whenever careful effort doesn't fully prevent a problem, 'but' captures that disappointing contrast.

quiz.question: Choose the correctly joined sentence:
options: I went to the store I bought milk.
I went to the store, and I bought milk.
I went to the store, bought milk.
I went, to the store I bought milk.
quiz.answer: I went to the store, and I bought milk.
quiz.explanation: Two complete sentences need a comma plus a joining word like 'and', not just a comma or nothing at all.

quiz.sentence: She was tired, she went to bed early.
quiz.correction: She was tired, so she went to bed early.
quiz.explanation: This is a comma splice - two complete sentences joined by only a comma need a connecting word like 'so'.

quiz.question: Which connecting word shows contrast between two ideas?
quiz.explanation: 'But' signals that the second idea contrasts with or contradicts the first.

quiz.question: It was raining, ___ we stayed inside. (shows result)
quiz.explanation: 'So' shows that the rain caused them to stay inside - a cause-and-effect relationship.

quiz.question: Which connecting word shows a reason or cause?
quiz.explanation: 'Because' introduces the reason or explanation for something.
